data.title: AMD: Multiple Red Flags Including Potential Multibillion Impairment Charge
data.description:
Introduction

I remain bearish on Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) not only because my previous 'Sell' call aged well but because fundamental problems keep snowballing. Problems with the working capital management are accumulating and there are no signs that the management has a clear roadmap of how to resolve this crisis. The latest earnings release revealed that the AI momentum for AMD is slowing down, which was the biggest catalyst that explained the stock's massive rally of 2023 and the first half of 2024. The gap with NVIDIA (NVDA) widens rapidly, which makes AMD very poorly positioned to compete with the GPU monster. There is also a massive $22.8 billion ticking-time bomb, which is the potential Xilinx-related goodwill impairment as Embedded segment's financial performance is falling off the cliff just three years after acquisition. The stock is massively overvalued, meaning that my take is a 'Strong Sell'. The reason why I am downgrading from just 'Sell' is due to the worsening situation around the working capital and the increasing risk of the multi-billion goodwill impairment charge in the Embedded segment.

Fundamental analysis

In my previous analysis, I emphasized big risks for AMD related to the mounting inventory. By the end of Q4, this problem became even bigger. The company's inventory balance grew to record $5.7 billion by the end of Q4, which is almost $400 million higher compared to Q3.

Nvidia's ending inventory balance is around 33% higher than AMD's, while its TTM revenue is almost 4.5 times higher ($113 billion vs $26 billion). This comparison screams about AMD's big inventory problem. Another indication that AMD struggles to manage working capital efficiently is the company's days sales outstanding. That is how long it takes them to get paid, which is almost 85 days. While AMD faces struggles to collect cash, Nvidia sits pretty at 41 days. This is also not a sign of a healthy and thriving business.

This working capital problem might have been less visible, should the AI craze for AMD continue. However, the recent earnings call demonstrated the market that peak growth rate levels for AMD's Data Center business are likely in the past. The company's AI revenue fell short of analysts' expectations, and the management did not provide near-term guidance for this part of the business during the latest earnings call.

Earnings revisions can give a lot of valuable insights to understand where a company is heading to. It might be even more useful if we compare trends in AMD's revisions with Nvidia. AMD saw many more EPS downgrades than upgrades for its next quarter's earnings, while Nvidia's quarterly earnings revisions look much stronger.

Other fundamental comparisons with Nvidia are also not in AMD's favor. AMD is very poorly positioned to compete against the undisputed AI leader from the balance sheet perspective. AMD's $2.8 billion net cash position looks like peanuts against Nvidia's $28 billion. The story for AMD is not pretty, as the balance sheet screams 'stay away' if you value financial strength.

The balance sheet factor cannot be underestimated because financial flexibility is the key to success in an extremely R&D-intensive industry like semiconductors. Nvidia is already miles ahead in terms of R&D spending and given the vast gap (which is already a canyon) in financial flexibility, AMD is extremely unlikely to ever catch up in terms of innovation.

Now, the most interesting part. There is another issue that looks like a ticking-time bomb to me. AMD's Embedded segment struggles to drive growth, with a 13% year-over-year decline in Q4. The story is even worse from the full-year angle, as the segment's revenue was down 33% compared to 2023. The segment's operating income decreased faster in 2024 vs 2023, at a 46% rate. I emphasize this because the segment appeared in AMD's financial reports as a separate line only after the $49 billion 'betting a farm' acquisition of Xilinx.

This acquisition took place just three years ago, and AMD's Embedded segment already struggles to drive sales. The rapidly evolving technological landscape with the emergence of AI capabilities might mean that this part of AMD's business is getting disrupted. Such an underperformance raises doubts on the management's strategic foresight when initiating such a large deal. I also believe that this entire situation raises large concerns about AMD's ability to recoup the massive $49 billion investment.

AMD's goodwill ballooned to almost $25 billion in early 2022. Out of them, $22.8 billion of goodwill is attributed to the Xilinx acquisition, which is a colossal risk given the Embedded segment's rapidly deteriorating financial performance. If this unfavorable trend continues, goodwill will become very close to the razor's edge. Even a 50% goodwill impairment charge will send the EPS deeply negative, since the company's largest ever net income of $3.2 billion (FY 2021) is way lower than the potential accounting implication.

Valuation analysis

The forward P/E ratio trajectory looks bullish at first glance. The FY2028 forward P/E ratio is ridiculously low for a semiconductor company with the AI exposure. The reason behind it is a very aggressive EPS growth over the next four years, which is questionable due to all the fundamental flaws.

Let's also look at the last three years' EBITDA margin performance, which is a key metric measuring operational efficiency of a business. Despite the fact that recent years were low-hanging fruits for companies with AI exposure, AMD's EBITDA margin has significantly dipped, and the recovery pace is quite slow. Therefore, I have doubts about AMD's ability to aggressively drive EPS growth, which will result in the significantly shrinking P/E ratio.

Thus, I would like to better rely on the outcome of the DCF model. I reiterate the same WACC of 9.4% as there were no swift changes in the Fed's monetary policy. Due to AMD's snowballing fundamental problems, I downgrade the constant growth rate to calculate the terminal value ('TV') from 6% to 5%.

The long-term revenue trajectory from consensus appears to be reliable as it incorporates revenue growth deceleration, which looks fair to me. The TTM levered FCF margin is 13.6%, and I use a subtle 25-basis point yearly expansion. I am not using a more aggressive improvement of the FCF due to the fierce competition from Nvidia, which will certainly weigh on AMD's pricing power. According to Seeking Alpha, there are 1.629 billion AMD shares outstanding.

AMD's fair share price is $90, significantly below the last close. The stock has a 19% downside potential, which reinforces my bearish outlook.

Mitigating factors

AMD's RSI indicator looks quite favorable as it stands at 41 at the moment. The level is not that far from the 'Oversold' zone, which might be a bullish indicator for some investors. The stock experienced quite a sharp decline over the last few months, which also means that some part of the market might be tempted to buy the dip.

We live in quite uncertain times, which might work in a positive way for AMD as well. Donald Trump's administration works on achieving peace between Russia and Ukraine, which might be a positive factor for all growth stocks. The reason is simple - eased sanctions against Russia's commodities is highly likely to have a massive global disinflationary effect, which will consequently result in faster-than-expected interest rate cuts from the Fed. This will lead to softer discount rates, which will have a positive effect on valuations of growth stocks, including AMD.

Nvidia is the undisputed leader of the entire global semiconductor industry, meaning that it sets trends. Thus, Nvidia's upcoming earnings release can be another positive catalyst for all big semiconductor names. Nvidia is set to release its earnings on February 26, which could potentially be a positive catalyst for AMD as well.

Conclusion

AMD's fundamental problems keep accumulating, and most of them will highly likely have a long-term adverse effect. The stock still has almost 20% downside potential, meaning that AMD is a no-brainer 'Strong Sell'.

data.title: Fastly: Despite The Strong Features, Cloudflare Is The Clear CDN Winner
data.description:
With the recent poor performance, investors are thinking about buying the Fastly’s (NYSE:FSLY) dip. For long term investors, I recommend holding. Given my reverse DCF analysis, with a dilution consideration, Fastly’s valuation is fairly valued. With low projected growth, stock returns should be low in the future. I rate Fastly a hold.

Background

Fastly core business is the service of Content Delivery Networks (CDN). CDN is very important to reduce latency, improving website processing and loading times. The computing of the website is simplified using a caching process while the website’s development is enriched with security features, reducing computing, latency and improving up-time. Low latency is very important for website usage. Users will churn from the website if it doesn’t perform or is slow to react. Less latency, especially, in e-commerce websites and other applications, such as gen AI, is very important to keep the user on the website engaging with the service.

One of the key features of this industry is very high entry barriers. To service a good CDN, companies have to invest significant capex in datacenters spread across multiple nations, requiring capital and bureaucracy. Thus, the network takes time to build and has to be combined with advanced software capabilities, for it to compete with other advanced CDN players.

Strategy

In this section, I would like to describe Fastly strategy in two main points, while mentioning its products and how they face against the major player called Cloudflare (NET).

High performing and technical CDN optimized for live websites. Fastly’s CDN is known to be very fast with good performance. Website development using Fastly’s framework is more powerful than Cloudflare’s. However, it also has a higher steeping curve than its main competitor. Fastly’s CDN is mostly used in e-commerce websites, streaming, and live websites that, for example, show live stock prices. Fastly’s enables very fast cache purging. To understand what is cache purging, first we need to understand what is cache. Cache temporary stores data from the previous website journeys. For example, if user 1 conducts a website journey, the journey data is temporarily stored in the CDN server. Then user 2 conducts the same journey with optimized web loading times because the CDN doesn’t have to load everything from the webserver, it already has in cache. Cache purging is the removal of a certain data item from cache, in order to load the updated data value from the web server. Fastly should have the fastest cache purging, of under 150 ms worldwide, compared to 1 second in Cloudflare’s service (data comes from a coding forum). This allows for better performance on live websites, such as e-commerce. In e-commerce, it is important to update fast the changing prices of goods, thus a fast cache purging is essential.

Take market share in the high-growth AI edge workload market. Fastly also provides services that improve the AI inferencing process through semantic caching. While traditional caching focuses on saving website data to improve website load times of frequent website journeys, semantic caching gives context to the temporary website data stored. If, for example, we are running a gen AI application, and we execute a prompt, semantic caching will understand the meaning of the prompt and upload a cached answer of a similar but not equal prompt. Thus, API calls, computing and latency are reduced with a small trade-off of reduced accuracy. Cloudflare, for now, doesn’t use semantic caching, but they plan to enable this feature in the future. Thus, Fastly has somewhat of a competitive advantage in its more advanced semantic caching. However, for now, I believe that the ChatGPT website is powered by Cloudflare, at least in my location, which is a negative for Fastly’s AI Accelerator. Fastly’s AI Accelerator is very recent, released at the end of 2024, needing more time to make an impact.

Overall, Cloudflare should have a more intuitive and simple, yet effective and more secure service than Fastly. Additionally, In the CDN segment, Cloudflare provides a SaaS subscription, rather than a pay-per-consumption used in Fastly. This allows for more scalability in Cloudflare’s users, a major competitive advantage for Cloudflare where enterprise customers represent most of the company’s revenue.

Competition from Cloudflare is strong, but Fastly is still able to provide differentiated features. Fastly should be able to absorb a significant part of the market under its service.

Financials

If we compare the gross margins, Cloudflare is much more profitable than Fastly. Thus, it has much more money to invest in R&D, Sales, and Capex. The gross margins being so different could suggest that Cloudflare has better management algorithms, teams and structure, providing a more cost-effective service.

The capex of Fastly in relation to revenue has been decelerating significantly, while Cloudflare’s remains stable. This could mean Fastly expects very low growth in the future and has less money to spend on capex than Cloudflare.

If we compare the price to gross profit, Cloudflare is 9 times more valued than Fastly. However, ignoring the valuation, Fastly is a much more risky investment than Cloudflare.

Finally, Fastly has slightly positive net debt (12% of gross profit) while Cloudflare has no net debt.

Opportunities

Fastly’s bot mitigation solution was released at the beginning of 2024, and now, according to the management, is creating cross-selling opportunities. Bot mitigation is very important, especially, for websites that run Ads. With the new programs, like, ChatGPT operator, that automate almost every step of buying an e-commerce product. If the website is running Ads, then they will be viewed by bots and not humans, reducing the website's advertisement appeal. More bots like these, with improved capabilities, will proliferate. It is important for some websites to have bot defenses.

Here is a comment from the company’s management in the Q3 2024 earnings call:

Let me share with you some important developments taking place in our security offerings in particular. We continue to experience favorable customer response from bot mitigation since its release in the first quarter. Our bot mitigation solution wholly developed within Fastly, attracts customers looking for simple onboarding and ease of use, opening the door for continued opportunities for cross-sell and upsell.

Risks

The cash and short-term investments have been quickly decreasing. To have money to invest in the business, such as capex and opex and investment in intangibles, Fastly could have to borrow money or dilute shareholders via stock offering. Making the business less attractive from an investment perspective.

Fastly has high competition from Cloudflare both in CDN’s and in advanced gen AI inferencing. Cloudflare, for now, doesn’t have the semantic caching process implemented but might have in the future.

Fastly customers top 10 customers represent a big chunk of the total revenue. If one churns, then it will highly impact Fastly.

Fastly SBC accounts for 8% of the market cap. We should expect a very high dilution, that combined with slow growth might affect the stock performance.

Valuation

To understand what growth the market is pricing, I like to do a reverse discounted cash flow analysis. To model the discount rate, I use 10%. The initial revenue value is the sell-side analysts estimate for 2025. The starting free cash flow margin is 5%. Then the margin expands to 11.79% in 2034. To calculate the terminal value, I considered a P/S of 1.7, considerably lower than the current 2.5 to account for dilution. Since I’m not doing the math per share and I wish to keep the model simple, I prefer to consider that the dilution affects the total market cap (It doesn’t, it just affects the price per share).

The model outputs a required growth of 7%. I believe the stock is fairly valued given sell-side analysts estimates. With a powerful competitor, in Cloudflare, high concentration of revenue and ongoing cash burn, Fastly investment becomes very risky.

Conclusion

Fastly has a differentiated offering compared to Cloudflare. Compared to other competition, Fastly has very high performance. However, its cost structure is not the best and the company is not increasing capex investments, affecting future growth and the renewal of the datacenters. Despite the interesting offerings, Fastly is in a weak position. I rate Fastly a hold.

data.title: AGCO's 2025 Outlook: Is A Turnaround On The Horizon?
data.description:
Introduction

Even in a bull market, we find winners and losers. If you've been a shareholder of AGCO Corporation (NYSE:AGCO) in the past year, you know what I mean: the stock is down 16%. And yet, the more it trades down, the higher its fwd PE goes. Why is that? Because investors expect AGCO's 2025 earnings to be low, way low. In this article, we will go over the industry and AGCO's latest financials to understand whether the stock may present an opportunity right now or not.

The industry

The truth is that the agriculture machinery manufacturing industry navigates in turmoiled waters. After a booming cycle from 2021 to 2023, boosted by strong crop prices and big government funding, the industry started reporting lower orders and, consequently, lower sales. In a few quarters, the strong pricing power many manufacturers claimed to have vanished, and we are now hearing that 2025 will be a year with flat pricing. Production hours are down across the board, and the 2025 outlook is weak.

As we recently learned from the three main players (controlling almost half of the market), Deere (DE), CNH Industrial (CNH), and AGCO, uncertain market conditions allowed the industry to tackle issues such as inventory management while optimizing their workforce count and protecting the companies' profitability.

Fundamentally, I am a bull of the industry and of agriculture in general. After all, there are good reasons why the tractor market is expected to grow from 2024 to 2030 at a CAGR between 3.5% to 5.9%.

Some secular trends fuel this forecast: the world population is increasing, thus leading to greater demand for food; many countries are transitioning to a more developed and sophisticated economy, and this changes eating behaviors, with a general increase in carb and protein demand; labor scarcity requires tractor manufacturers to develop autonomous solutions; arable land is every day rarer, and this augments the urgency for precision ag.

In such an environment, I believe there is only one real strategy, and I moved out of CNH Industrial and invested in the market leader John Deere. When demand softens, and we prepare for a rebound, it is usually the leader that benefits first and, for longers, the gradual improvement. However, when Deere jumped 8% after its Q4 earnings were not as bad as expected, I decided to sell the stock at a price of around $450. At that time, I also sold AGCO at a price of $95. I took this choice because I thought the stocks were becoming too expensive, even when factoring in an anticipated late 2025 rebound. As things stand now, 2025 will be a tough year and, perhaps, the trough of this cycle. Deere's quarterly sales are now down over 15% compared to three years ago (and this chart still doesn't reflect the recent 1Q25 report, with quarterly sales down to only $3B vs. $5.2 two years ago).

Well, let's take a closer look at AGCO's latest earnings report and assess what is going on with a company whose brand portfolio comprises a world-class brand such as Fendt.

AGCO's Q4 Earnings Review

If you're interested in the industry, you are probably already aware of the main financials that AGCO reported. However, to make this article easier to read for everyone, allow me to recap a few key numbers:

  • AGCO's FY24 net sales of $11.7B were down 19% YoY; quarterly sales were down 24% to $2.9B.
  • The geographic breakdown of revenues sees North America down 38.7%, while Europe was down "only" 16.7%. These are AGCO's two main markets. The other key market is South America, which was also down 31.6%.
  • Adj. operating margin was 8.9% (-310bps YoY), back to high-single-digits; quarterly operating margin was 9.9% (-80bps YoY).
  • AGCO cut production hours by a third in Q4 and managed to reduce its inventory levels below those reached in 2023.
  • adj. EPS were down significantly by 52% to $7.50.
  • FCF was $297M (FY23: $585M).

That the year had not been exactly a great one was known, so what truly mattered about this earnings report was the answer that AGCO gave to the question everyone has: how long will this market environment last?

Well, first and foremost, AGCO immediately told us production hours in 2025 will be 15% to 20% lower versus 2024 when they ended up being 28% down vs. 2023. This suggests that AGCO expects 2025 to be worse than 2024. In fact, if we look at its market outlook, we immediately understand where the problem lies. While Western Europe will be slightly down and Brazil should be flat, North America should see large ag tractor sales reduced by a quarter.

This was more or less confirmed by AGCO's competitors: 2025 won't be the year of the North American market. In fact, we also learned that AGCO sees livestock-focused farms still reporting strong income while crop-focused farms are being weakened by volatile crop prices. This means that farmers won't likely invest in costly tractors or combines. This will be further reinforced by the fact that everyone is expecting more interest rate cuts down the road, so it seems that 2026 might be a more favorable year to take a loan to finance a costly equipment purchase.

True, the Farm Capital Investment Index recently released by Purdue University suggests that farmer sentiment is increasing, but we are still quite below the average of the past decade.

So, when we read that AGCO expects 2025 net sales below the psychological level of $10B ($9.6B to be exact), with the adj. EPS taking another big hit. The forecast is in the range between $4.00 to $4.50. This would represent another 40+% drop YoY. Let's say it right away. Many investors greatly appreciated AGCO's special dividend ($4.00 in 2021, $4.50 in 2022, $5.00 in 2023, $2.50 in 2024), which will be minimal, and it might also not be announced. This sensibly lowers the dividend yield, which has somewhat helped the stock price.

But we are also before major EPS reductions that are making AGCO's valuation quite strange. During the peaks of the last cycle, AGCO was trading at 10x its earnings. Now, while the share prices have come down a bit, its earnings multiples are close to 24. This means AGCO is absurdly priced at a multiple in line with the overall market. But the market is in bull mode, and multiples are expanding because of positive expectations on future earnings; AGCO's business is softening, and this leads to a very high multiple that makes the company even more costly. Moreover, if AGCO's earnings do drop by at least 40% this year, we would need to expect a very robust 2026 with 50%+ EPS growth to talk about a 2026 fwd PE of 15.5. Now, it is hard to believe that the rebound will be as strong and quick as if we were in 2021.

As a result, although I believe the company is good and strong, I also consider the current valuation too optimistic compared to the data we are gathering. This creates a situation where there isn't much margin of safety to start a position. Therefore, I consider the stock a hold right now. It is better to wait a few more quarters to understand whether the strong 2026 recovery the markets are expecting will truly begin to dawn.

data.title: Series A Preferred Shares Of Popular, Inc. Offer Stable Monthly Income
data.description:
Herein, I revisit a prior article I wrote for Seeking Alpha concerning Popular, Inc. (NASDAQ:BPOP) (the “Bank”); in particular, the Bank’s 6.375% Noncumulative Monthly Income Preferred Stock, 2003 Series A (the "Series A Preferred Shares"), which trade under the ticker OTCPK:BPOPO.

The Puerto Rican banking sector is often overlooked and in the past I have covered the sector, including OFG Bancorp (OFG), and First BanCorp (FBP), in addition to the Bank. Much of what I have written in the past concerning the sector still rings true, including the following:

Reasons to consider an investment in Puerto Rican Banks include: [1] with only a handful of major banks, the banking market in Puerto Rico is oligopolistic. . .; [2] mainland U.S. banks have generally not looked to expand into Puerto Rico, making it a less competitive market (buttressing the oligopoly trend, which could lead to increasing margins in the long run); [3] Puerto Rican banks have proved resilient (even if the markets are skeptical) despite Hurricane Maria, economic and political crises and... COVID-19…; [4] the negative effects of, and malaise caused by, Puerto Rico's government debt restructuring, of which these banks generally do not have any exposure...; and [5] longer term, the island may benefit from onshoring, particularly in the pharmaceutical and medical device industries, which are well established on the island."

While there are risks of investing in the Series A Preferred Shares, and while I am currently generally adverse to income securities other than low duration debt, I have started to build a small position. The small exposure in the Series A Preferred Shares will help goose my cash returns which have declined along with the Federal Reserve's interest rate cuts (100 basis point thus far in the current rate cutting cycle, which is likely over for now due to accelerating inflation).

At or near $24.80, the Series A Preferred Shares are yielding near 6.50% compared to money markets yielding about 4.25%.

Popular, Inc. Background

Founded in 1893, the Bank is, according to its Investor Relations web page, a full-service financial services provider with operations in Puerto Rico, the United States, and Virgin Islands. BPOP is the largest banking institution by both assets and deposits in Puerto Rico, and in the United States the Bank ranks among the top thrifts by total assets. According to Investor Relations (linked above), the Bank's operations include:

Banco Popular de Puerto Rico (BPPR) – With branches in Puerto Rico and the Virgin Islands, BPPR offers retail and commercial banking services, as well as auto and equipment leasing and financing, mortgage loans, insurance, investment banking and broker-dealer services.

Popular Bank – Popular Bank provides financial expertise to enable customers to focus on what matters most. Leveraging its institutional depth, Popular Bank works side-by-side with business and retail customers delivering competitive business and retail offerings including Retail and Private Banking, Commercial Lending, Mortgage Services, Association Lending, Healthcare Financing and Middle Market Banking. Popular Bank has branches in New York, New Jersey and Florida, 24/7 digital banking solutions and access to 55,000 ATMs worldwide...."

Insider Buying and Common Equity Performance

Back when I first wrote about the Bank, there was no insider selling and modest insider buying of the Bank's stock. Over the last year, however, there have been no insider buys and several insider sales. Intuitively, this makes sense as the Bank's common stock price has roughly doubled, and the Bank has outperformed the S&P 500, since my prior article concerning the Bank (i.e., since November 24, 2020). Impressive performance by the Bank, and I kick myself for not participating in the common equity.

The Series A Preferred Shares

Originally issued in 2003, BPOP's Series A Preferred Shares include annual dividends of $1.59375 per share, payable monthly, if declared by the Bank's board of directors. Similar to common equity dividends, these dividends never have to be paid.

The Series A Preferred Shares include a five-year no-call period, which has long since expired; as such, the Series A Preferred Shares are redeemable at any time at the option of BPOP, but with the shares being perpetual (i.e., no maturity date), there is no obligation for BPOP ever to redeem the shares.

At the current price below $25, there is a modest premium for the holders of Series A Preferred Shares should the Bank choose to redeem them. Having been outstanding for more than 20 years, however, I can only surmise that the Bank has no present intention of redeeming.

The monthly dividend is $0.1328 per share and has been paid without interruption since the shares were first issued in 2003 (i.e., more than 20 years of consecutive dividend payments). Absent a cataclysmic event, I expect dividends to be paid for the next 20 years if the Bank does not sooner redeem the Series A Preferred Shares.

The Series A Preferred Shares are NOT cumulative, however. This means that not only do monthly dividends not have to be paid if the Board so elects, but if monthly payments are missed, the BPOP Board of Directors has no obligation to make up any shortfall in the future. Again, while a risk, it is probably one that is remote given the stability of the Bank.

The Series A Preferred Shares are not liquid, with only 825 shares exchanging hands each day on average according to the Seeking Alpha quote page; thus, using limit orders is absolutely necessary when trading these preferred shares.

Normally, I would not be inclined to invest in non-cumulative, non-liquid preferred shares, but such shares are not unusual in the highly regulated banking sector. Moreover, as noted above, these shares yield near 6.5% at the current price, dividends are paid monthly, and the Bank's financial performance has generally improved over the last few years - see below (and for this reason, the Bank's confident management recently increased its common equity dividend by nearly 13%).

Metric November 24, 2020 February 13, 2024
Price of Series A Preferred Shares $25.49 $24.79
Common Equity Price $51.51 $103.00
Common Equity Tier 1 Capital Ratio** 15.90% 16.03%
Tangible Book Value $61.69 $68.16
Dividend Yield 6.25% 6.48%
Common Stock Dividend $1.60/sh. for 2020 $2.80/sh. for 2025

**Compares favorably to the largest banks.

Since I last covered the Series A Preferred Shares, the price of the shares have declined, causing the dividend yield to rise. The common shares have doubled. Moreover, as noted above, the common equity dividend has risen substantially. Finally, as shown above, the Bank's Common Equity Tier 1 Capital ratio has strengthened over the last several years, as has its Tangible Book Value.

Should the Bank suffer liquidity problems, it could cut the common dividend to pay its preferred shareholders; it could also issue more common equity. In addition, it could also end common share buybacks, which totaled nearly $60 million for the nine months ended September 30, 2024 per the most recent 10-Q. Of course, other measures could be taken, but there is no guaranty that the Bank would take any measures to protect the holders of Series A Preferred Shares. That said, such risks should be balanced against the fact that the Bank continued to pay dividends on the Series A Preferred Shares during the entirety of the Great Financial Crisis.

Concluding Thoughts

The Series A Preferred Shares are not for every investor. Priced near $24.80, however, the Series A Preferred Shares offer monthly income of nearly 6.50%, which, in my view is reasonable compensation relative to the risks associated with the non-cumulative nature of these preferred securities (as well as the other risks included in this article and the prospectus), particularly as a very small allocation of my overall portfolio.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: All-In: The 23-Stock Six-Figure Dividend Portfolio I'm Betting My Future On
data.description:
Introduction

Before I do anything else, I want to use this opportunity to thank the entire Seeking Alpha community for their support.

Roughly 11 months ago, I wrote a 30,000-follower special article, celebrating a major milestone in my follower count. Since then, that number has risen to more than 40,000, a number I never expected to reach when I started writing for this website roughly a decade ago.

Hence, from the bottom of my heart, I want to thank every follower and reader for their support! I could not possibly ask for a more supportive community, which adds tremendous value by commenting, bringing up suggestions, and providing criticism where needed.

With that said, over the past few weeks, I promised readers I would write another portfolio article, revealing my 23-stock dividend portfolio.

Although I use almost every article I write to give people a closer look into what goes on in my brain, I don't often discuss my entire portfolio. This is due to a number of reasons:

  • I own just 23 single stocks and discuss most of them on a regular basis.
  • My portfolio turnover is very low, meaning I rarely sell or add stocks.
  • Unlike many others, I decided to not track every single aspect of my portfolio. I believe in keeping it as simple as possible to spend most of my energy on things that matter, which is finding the right investments and giving them the appropriate weighting in my portfolio.

Nonetheless, occasional portfolio updates are important, as they allow me to give people a full overview of my portfolio, including major changes and my thoughts with regard to new additions and potential consolidation.

So, as we have a lot to discuss, let's get to it!

On a side note, you can access my prior portfolio review here.

A Little Bit About Me

I'm 29. I started investing when I was 16.

However, back then, it wasn't a very serious endeavor, as I had little money, a huge ego, and no clue about anything. The good thing about this mix is that I was humbled quickly, making every mistake a young investor can make without losing life-changing money. Back then, it felt different, but now I'm very grateful for that period.

At 18-19, I got a great mentor who introduced me to macroeconomic research, something I was always interested in. However, he taught me how "the pros" use macro research, which was one of the most important lectures I ever got.

Over the years, I completed a master's degree in international business with a focus on purchasing and supply management. This degree was extremely valuable, as I believe understanding global supply chains is critical when making investment decisions.

Currently, a lot of my research is based on these issues, including energy (i.e., pipelines), economic re-shoring, tariff trades, and many others.

During the last few months of my master's program, I started to work for a small team that advised a hedge fund manager in the U.S., who had an elevated nine-figure net worth and a partner position in a hedge fund focused on finding underappreciated and hidden opportunities.

This was my first serious job that allowed me to apply some of my knowledge and refine my own investment approach. After all, seeing how some of the best in the world were looking at stocks really opened my eyes.

All of this happened around 2020/2021 when I became serious about dividend growth investing. At that time, I closed all trading accounts and decided to invest all of my excess capital in dividend (growth) stocks.

Since then, I have done exactly that.

Currently, I have 91% of my entire net worth invested in 23 dividend stocks. My net worth is in the higher-six-digit range, which means I can retire in a wide range of cheap nations - if I wanted. I'm obviously not doing that, but it's fun to know that if I wanted, I could.

Also, it helped me to focus a lot more on retired investors in my articles, as I am consistently thinking of portfolio constructions that could substantially boost my portfolio income.

Moreover, while my portfolio is nothing to brag about, I put "six digits" in the title because it's too much money to make stupid mistakes. Because I invest everything in my favorite stocks, I am extremely picky, as my entire financial future relies on these stocks.

My Strategy

When it comes to putting money to work, I do not use fancy models or advanced strategies. My investment approach is very straightforward, as I simply want to own the best companies. The better the business, the more capital I will deploy.

I like to use what I call an "ownership view." I put myself in the shoes of the company and always ask myself if I would buy the entire company if I could (Buffett-style). I want to understand what makes companies special and find the ones I literally want to hold on forever.

When I started investing, I wanted to keep an average yield of roughly 3%. These plans have gone out the window, as I realized I'm too young to focus on income. In fact, if I want to retire, I will just sell lower-yielding stocks and make some portfolio shifts. As I live in a country that doesn't have capital gains (it has a whole lot of other taxes), that strategy works for me.

With that said, whenever I pick stocks, I also like to apply my "big picture" framework, which is built on macroeconomic developments, politics, supply chains, and smaller factors like market sentiment, valuations, and economic cycles.

In almost all of my articles, I want to highlight at least some of these areas, as it shows readers where my mind is at and because it provides food for thought, even if people dislike my stock picks - there's something for everyone.

Based on this context, this is what I wrote in my prior portfolio overview when it comes to picking the right stocks:

  • I am looking for companies with wide-moat business models, preferably with "mission-critical" operations in the supply chains they operate in. This includes railroads, defense contractors/aerospace suppliers, healthcare equipment suppliers, stock exchange operators, pipeline operators, and many more.
  • I am looking for consistent dividend growth, preferably supported by buybacks. Historically speaking, dividend growth stocks are the best-performing equities (see below). However, I do not necessarily care for Dividend Aristocrats and Dividend Kings. Although a strong dividend track record is important, the additional value that comes with uninterrupted dividend growth fades over time (see this article). To me, it is no problem if a company refrains from growing its dividend during a recession. As long as the long-term dividend trend is up (without cuts), I'm happy.

With that said, while I do not care for a specific yield when investing, I still use the pyramid approach I brought up in a number of articles. Essentially, it helps investors structure a portfolio, as they need to put stocks in very specific categories:

  • Growth: These are often low-yielding dividend stocks with elevated growth. Buying a low yield is not a problem as long as the longer-term growth outlook is good. The biggest risk here is a steep decline in growth, which could hurt capital gains.
  • Mix: This group of stocks has both decent yields (often 2-3%) and consistently elevated dividend growth in the high-single-digit to low-double-digit range. Broadly speaking, this is my favorite place to pick stocks from.
  • Income: These stocks provide elevated income and often come with subdued growth. Here, the biggest risks are buying "sucker yields," which are companies that are simply not competitive anymore. This can result in dividend cuts and capital losses. While buying elevated income is perfectly fine, the focus here needs to be on safety and consistency, as I will want dividend growth rates to exceed the expected rate of inflation.

With that said, let's take a closer look at my portfolio.

My 23-Stock Portfolio

Let me start this part of the article by showing you a chart that may shock you. The overview below shows the sector breakdown of my portfolio.

As you can see, nearly 80% of my entire portfolio is invested in energy and industrial stocks. Real estate and healthcare account for a combined 11%.

As a comparison, the S&P 500 has just 3% energy and 8% industrial exposure. It has 33% information technology exposure. I have 0% information technology exposure.

Now, before you call me crazy, allow me to show you another chart.

The next pie chart shows the industry breakdown of my portfolio.

As we can see, my biggest investments are in oil and gas and aerospace and defense. These two areas account for roughly half of my exposure.

I know what you're thinking right now. That still looks bad, right?

However, these 34% aren't really oil and gas investments. These 34% are my two biggest investments in Texas Pacific Land Corp. (TPL) and LandBridge (LB). On December 16, I covered these two ideas in a special article, which explained that we're dealing with two landowners in the Permian Basin.

These two companies own land. This land comes with oil and gas mineral rights, water rights, and surface rights. In other words, these two make money on everything that happens on and under their land.

  • They get oil and gas royalties without spending a penny on CapEx.
  • They benefit from the booming demand for water infrastructure, as the Delaware Basin comes with up to ten barrels of water per produced barrel of oil. They make money on every gallon of water flowing on and through their land.
  • They make money from resource sales.
  • Even more important, because natural gas and water are so abundant in the Permian Basin, they are becoming increasingly suitable for commercial applications like data centers, hydrogen plants, crypto miners, and many others.

This makes these two stocks so superior. In other words, my cyclical energy exposure is really low. In fact, I own just one company that drills for oil and gas. That company is Canadian Natural (CNQ), which is a giant in Canada's low-cost oil sands.

With that said, here's my entire portfolio:

As we can see below, some of my favorite positions have become my smallest investments - despite consistent buying. The good news is that this offers opportunities. In the months ahead, I'm planning on buying a lot more PepsiCo due to the decline in consumer staples.

I will also be buying a lot more Danaher and Canadian Pacific, as I believe these low-yielding dividend growers have what it takes to generate outperforming capital gains for many years - if not decades - to come.

Moreover, I'm planning on consolidating some of my holdings. I would like to reduce my defense exposure from five companies to three. The reason I own five is because all of them bring something special to the table.

  • Lockheed is a "higher-yielding" leader that has major exposure in programs like the F-35.
  • L3Harris and Northrop Grumman have bigger supplier roles, which provides diversification.
  • RTX Corp. is a highly diversified producer of commercial and defense aerospace products. This includes Pratt & Whitney engines, Collins supplies for countless commercial and defense programs, and Raytheon missile defense.
  • GE Aerospace is a commercial-focused engine producer with high-growth rates and fantastic management.

The only reason I haven't consolidated this sector yet is that I believe all of these companies trade far below their fair value. However, if LMT moves toward $600, I will likely sell it and put the proceeds into the other four. As much as I love LMT, I want to gradually move toward supplier-focused defense companies.

I am also looking to go from three railroads to two. However, I haven't done so, as I believe all three are undervalued. Going from three to two is a long-term plan I could see myself executing once the cyclical economy gets overheated. At that point, it would make sense to sell one of the three.

However, for now, I expect the exact opposite to happen, as I wrote in a recent article.

This brings me to the dividend.

My Portfolio's Dividend Yield

My portfolio yields 1.9%. This excludes special dividends, which are usually paid by CME Group, Texas Pacific, and Canadian Natural.

  • The S&P 500 yields 1.2%.
  • The Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF (VIG) yields 1.7%.

Although I never planned to reach 1.8%, it makes sense, as I mainly target "mix" stocks in the dividend pyramid we briefly discussed earlier in this article.

The majority of my dividends are paid at the end of each calendar quarter, meaning March, June, September, and December. I estimate current annual dividend growth to be in the high-single-digit growth rate, fueled by Texas Pacific Land, LandBridge, and my investments in fast-growing companies like GE Aerospace and Carlisle Companies.

With that said, my dividend is highly concentrated. After all, a third of my portfolio (my biggest two investments) barely pay any regular dividends.

Industrials, energy, and real estate account for the vast majority of my dividend payments. This is led by Antero Midstream, which accounts for 14% of my entire dividend income. Canadian Natural accounts for 10% of my total income. I need to take this into account if I want to focus on income. Being too dependent on a single company is risky, even if it's a safe company - especially once we are fully dependent on our dividend income.

Here's an overview of the total contribution per holding. Please note that this excludes special dividends.

With that said, just like in my last portfolio breakdown, I want to share some thoughts on the future.

Outperformance & Thoughts On The Future

Even though I do not own technology stocks that have propelled the market to new highs since the start of the AI revolution, I have beaten the market.

Over the past 12 months alone, I have generated a return of 48%, more than the 20% return of the S&P 500. This performance was skewed by the fantastic performance of Texas Pacific Land and LandBridge, which became my best investments ever due to elevated returns and my decisions to go massively overweight.

Last year also caused my total return since 2021 to rise to more than 140%, as we can see below. Please note that the chart below has no x-axes. On the left is August 2021. Back then, I switched brokers.

While it may sound arrogant, I have little doubt I will be able to beat the market going forward, as I expect two big tailwinds to support me:

  • Potentially bottoming cyclical growth.
  • A rotation from growth to value.

Especially the last bullet point is key and something we have discussed in countless articles. After years of underperformance for value stocks, I expect broadening economic growth and the market's lofty valuation to result in a rotation to stocks with better valuations.

This would be great news, as I believe most of my stocks have very attractive multiples, supported by secular growth tailwinds, healthy balance sheets, and consistent dividend growth.

As we can see below, in 2021 value stocks bottomed relative to growth stocks. Although growth has regained some growth, the past few years were unable to push growth to new relative highs. This is despite falling inflation and AI tailwinds. With bottoming inflation and maturing AI, I believe the risk/reward massively shifts in favor of value stocks.

Although I am by no means saying that growth is dead, I believe the time is perfect to own high-quality dividend (growth) stocks. Also, needless to say, I'll continue to spend a lot of time discussing these developments in the months and quarters ahead.

In general, I'll continue to use my daily articles to elaborate on portfolio picks, macroeconomic developments, and future investment plans, as it's very hard to cover everything in great detail in a single article.

So, stay tuned for that!

Takeaway

Reaching 40,000 followers on Seeking Alpha is a milestone I never imagined when I started writing a decade ago, and I'm deeply grateful for this supportive community.

Today, I shared my 23-stock dividend portfolio, which reflects my focus on quality, simplicity, and long-term growth.

With 91% of my net worth invested in these stocks, I prioritize wide-moat businesses, consistent dividend growth, and macroeconomic trends.

While my portfolio is heavily concentrated in energy and industrials, it's designed to weather cycles and capitalize on undervalued opportunities.

Moreover, despite avoiding tech, I've outperformed the market, and I remain confident in my strategy as I expect a rotation to value stocks.

All things considered, thank you for your continued support! I look forward to sharing more insights and growing our portfolios together!

data.title: Janus Henderson Global Sustainable Equity (ADR) Managed Account Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment environment

The fourth quarter marked an underwhelming end to a strong year for global equity markets, with the MSCI World Index posting a small negative return (in U.S. dollars). Donald Trump’s win in the U.S. presidential election sparked a rally in U.S. equities in November, although his threats of igniting a global trade war meant markets lagged elsewhere – most notably in Europe. December was a weak month for risk assets, with the bond market showing signs of caution. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) increased to just over a six-month high after the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) projected fewer rate cuts for 2025 than previously expected. There were multiple elections around the world during the period, with over 70 countries going to the polls. But the U.S. presidential election dominated the narrative. The rally following Trump’s victory was spurred by investors embracing the concept of deregulation, extended tax cuts, and a generally more pro-business administration. Cryptocurrencies and shares in banks and Tesla (TSLA) – given Elon Musk's close relationship with Trump for the latter – outperformed. Meanwhile, the potential for higher import tariffs, reductions in subsidies for green initiatives, and healthcare reform meant that companies with complex supply chains, clean energy solutions, or healthcare exposure generally underperformed.

As had been the case throughout 2024, market narrowness prevailed over the period. The Magnificent Seven, comprising large tech companies Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), Alphabet (GOOG,GOOGL), and Tesla, contributed over 50% to the S&P 500® Index’s (SP500, SPX) return and finished the year representing almost 30% of the index. These companies’ strength largely stemmed from their leadership in artificial intelligence. Given this backdrop, consumer discretionary, communication services, and information technology were the best-performing sectors. The materials sector fared the worst, pressured by initial uncertainty about stimulus measures in China, a strong U.S. dollar, and anxiety about possible U.S. trade tariffs. Healthcare underperformed due to possible looming healthcare reform. Real estate weakened as bond yields rose and investors scaled back their U.S. rate cut expectations.

Portfolio review

The portfolio’s lack of investment in the majority of Magnificent Seven tech stocks was a major driver of relative underperformance over the period, given these stocks dominated index returns.

Stock selection also drove relative underperformance. Two of our healthcare holdings, Icon (ICLR) and Humana (HUM), suffered significant share price declines. UBER also detracted as the market rewarded Elon Musk, sending Tesla’s share price higher on optimism for a potential easing of autonomous vehicle regulations, and seemed to regard Uber as a potential resulting loser – which is not a view we share. Our renewable energy holdings also hurt relative returns as market sentiment soured toward this sector. Elsewhere, our European industrial investments lagged in reaction to Trump’s aggressive rhetoric on trade tariffs, which also negatively impacted several of our Japanese companies including cycling components manufacturer Shimano (OTCPK:SHMDF), another top detractor.

While some of our holdings outperformed the benchmark, their outperformance was insufficient to offset the detractors. In communication services, Spotify (SPOT) and T-Mobile (TMUS) were key contributors, as were Wabtec in industrials and McKesson (MCK) in healthcare.

The largest contributors to relative returns were audio streaming service Spotify, drug wholesale distributor McKesson, and network telecommunications company T- Mobile.

Spotify announced healthy third-quarter results, showing growth in subscribers, monthly average users, and free- cash-flow generation. The company announced further product capabilities, including video podcasts. The recent results and business announcements underscore our belief that the company is on the cusp of a shift in relative power in the music industry, and we think this should translate into better business economics for Spotify. The company is aligned with our “Quality of Life” theme in that it has revolutionized how the world enjoys music, podcasts, audiobooks, and other forms of content, enabling greater accessibility and enhancing discovery, while democratizing music distribution.

McKesson rebounded in the fourth quarter after releasing a robust set of results and delivering an optimistic sell-side update. Earlier in 2024, the company also announced a deal that will bolster its leading efforts in oncology support and solutions. Longer term, we believe McKesson has a leading position in a consolidated market with defensive growth characteristics. We think it is well placed to benefit from secular tailwinds in biosimilar, specialty, and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) products.

T-Mobile reported solid third-quarter results, beating estimates and raising its full-year guidance. T-Mobile is the clear leader in 5G investment in the U.S., where its 5G networks cover almost the entire U.S. As one of the best cable and wireless companies in terms of execution and innovation, we believe the company will play a pivotal role in the upcoming era of ubiquitous connectivity through the Internet of Things.

Among the biggest detractors on a relative basis were clinical research company ('CRO') Icon, healthcare insurer Humana, and ride-hailing platform Uber Technologies.

Shares in Icon fell after the company released its third- quarter results, which were below guidance previously provided by its management, with revenues falling notably short of expectations. The company’s CEO cited ongoing cautiousness from biotech customers as a primary reason, and although this is expected to persist into 2025, he noted that recent new strategic partnerships had already contributed to investment pipelines that underpin the fundamental strength of the business. As a CRO, Icon helps pharmaceutical companies manage the human trial process that supports the development of new drugs and devices that save lives, as well as improve quality of life. Our long-term thesis is unchanged.

Disappointingly, Humana continued to underperform, having suffered a downgrade in its star rating, which affects its eligibility for quality bonuses. However, the company is taking legal action against this downgrade. Humana saw further weakness in its shares due to reports of a risk to earnings coming from members who utilized both Medicare Advantage and the Veterans Health Administration cover.

We continue to believe that in the longer term, Humana, as the dominant provider of Medicare Advantage plans, is well exposed to aging demographics in the U.S. The firm is a pioneer in integrated care and aims to lower costs by encouraging healthy choices that prevent future medical problems.

Uber also underperformed. In October, Tesla revealed its first “robotaxi” – a self-driving car that operates as a taxi. Meanwhile, Waymo, a ride-hailing service entirely focused on autonomous driving, saw increased ride volumes and expanded its service areas. This raised concerns about Uber’s role in a world of autonomous vehicles. The near-term backdrop is also challenged, with Trump’s stance on immigration presenting a potential headwind to Uber, as well as Trump’s close relationship with Elon Musk. While the near-term could be challenging, this does not change our long-term investment thesis. As the leading ride-sharing platform globally, Uber continues to have solid fundamentals. Uber’s ride-sharing products allow riders to reduce their travel costs while driving a higher utilization of vehicles, fueling a circular economy dynamic. In addition, Uber has committed to being a fully electric, zero-emission platform by 2030 in Canada, Europe, and the U.S., and globally by 2040.

Manager outlook

Notwithstanding the outsized contribution of the Magnificent Seven, 2024 encouragingly saw the beginning of a broadening in equity returns. We expect this trend to continue, supported by growing confidence in an economic soft landing – particularly in the U.S. – and changing geopolitical dynamics.

We think that the market's broadening trend in 2025 presents a highly favorable landscape for active investors. While the Magnificent Seven have dominated the S&P 500 Index, their disproportionate influence has created potential value opportunities in other market segments. As such, we are remaining watchful for attractive entry points in overlooked areas, both in the U.S. and globally. The combination of potentially lower interest rates and projected strong earnings growth could further enhance these opportunities, in our view.

The U.S. presidential election presents intriguing investment avenues, but also introduces uncertainties for global markets, particularly regarding potential punitive tariffs and U.S. dollar strengthening. While sustainability-related investment themes may face challenges under the Trump administration, historical precedent suggests that fears may not match reality. For example, the U.S. added more renewables during the previous Trump presidency than during the Obama administration. Significantly, companies tend to operate on longer timelines than presidential terms, so we think that these corporates are likely to maintain their commitment to sustainability. Moreover, current attractive valuations across the green-asset spectrum could lead to better-than-expected performance in the sector.

Earnings growth is anticipated globally in 2025, with productivity gains driven by AI applications and innovation potentially differentiating company performance. Sectors beyond IT, such as healthcare, are seeing groundbreaking innovations that could drive returns. An example is GLP-1 drugs for obesity, which are already annualizing more than $50 billion in sales and have been growing by 50% annually. This is just one example of the many innovations that we think could drive returns in the year ahead and lead to a more dynamic playing field for equity investors.

Regional factors will also play a role in 2025. Europe may see deeper interest rate cuts, potentially benefiting risk assets. Japan's corporate reforms and end of deflation could support its stock market. India's continued pro-manufacturing and infrastructure policies under Prime Minister Narendra Modi's reelection may boost growth. And China's recent stimulus efforts could help stabilize its equity market and lift economic growth.

Ultimately, our investment approach remains steadfastly grounded in bottom-up stock selection. We employ a rigorous and consistent methodology, emphasizing thorough fundamental analysis. Our focus is on identifying companies with compelling financial profiles that we believe have the potential for long-term wealth creation while making the world a better place. Throughout this process, we maintain strict adherence to valuation discipline.

We recognize that the ongoing economic and technological transition, which is deeply intertwined with sustainability themes, represents a significant multiyear megatrend that is likely to persist. To fully leverage the opportunities presented by this shift, we believe adopting a long-term perspective is crucial. Through this approach we seek to navigate short- term market fluctuations while positioning our portfolio to benefit from enduring, transformative trends in the global economy.

Portfolio management

Hamish Chamberlayne, CFA | Aaron Scully, CFA

data.title: Roku Q4 Earnings: Look Beyond The Noise, Inflection Point
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Roku (NASDAQ:ROKU) is on the cusp of a turnaround story. I was wrong to have been neutral on this stock.

Looking ahead to 2025, Roku is expecting its free cash flow to nearly jump 100% y/y. This is going to get a lot of institutional investors to back into this stock and buy this fallen angel.

Presently, including the after-hours jump, the stock is priced at 35x forward free cash flow and it operates debt-free. There's a lot to be bullish about here.

Rapid Recap

In my previous analysis, prior to earnings, I called this stock wrong. I said,

I find myself on the fence on this stock. It could work out, but it may just as easily not work out. Its outlook is too mixed for me.

On the back of these latest results, I upgrade my rating to a buy.

Roku's Near-Term Prospects

Roku is a steaming TV platform. Roku's operating system is built into connected TVs, allowing over 90 million households to watch TV shows, as well as apps like Netflix (NFLX), Disney+ (DIS), and YouTube (GOOG) (GOOGL).

Looking back, it's difficult to believe that this stock has fallen more than 80% from its Covid highs.

However, if I think about it earnestly, I suspect it makes sense. After all, many investors were using Roku's TV platform during Covid, and so, they became very familiar with the company. And a familiarity bias portends itself to the stock trading at a high multiple.

Even though, in actual fact, the money that Roku makes is from ads and subscriptions to the platform, not from selling devices, of course.

Indeed, as Roku guides ahead, they state that they are expecting its ads business to grow even faster than 2024.

Furthermore, to expand its reach, Roku is also expanding its retail reach for Roku-branded TVs by selling in places like Walmart, Target, and Amazon. If things go as planned, Roku should keep adding users and increasing its revenue.

Given this background, let's now delve into its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates Could Reach 15% Y/Y

In my previous analysis, I said,

There's good news and bad news. The good news is that Roku still has substantial topline growth. That is very much supportive of the bull thesis.

The bad news is that next year's comparables will be tough, which probably translates into Roku growing at 15% CAGR and no more, which will mean a deceleration from 2024. On top of that, recall that in 2025 there will be no election-advertising tailwinds.

Even though Roku is guiding for about 12% topline growth in 2025, I suspect that this is management lowballing estimates to a certain extent and that its growth rates will be largely in line with my previous expectations.

The good news though, is that growing by 15% y/y on the back of 18% y/y revenue growth delivered in 2024 starts to build substantial investor confidence, that beyond the noise, Roku still has a fair amount of staying power and growth left in its engine.

With this in mind, let's discuss its valuation.

ROKU Stock Valuation -- 35x Forward Free Cash Flow

In my previous analysis, I said,

As an Inflection investor, I recognize, as I've done for a long time, that the bull case for Roku is supported by its steel-like balance sheet. More specifically, more than 20% of its market cap is made up of cash and cash equivalents.

Indeed, it's difficult to be too bearish on ROKU when the business holds so much cash! I believe that cash on a balance sheet is like oxygen. You don't really care to think about it most of the time. Apart from when you need it. And when you need it, you really need it. And on that front, ROKU fares very well.

Given that its share price has moved up slightly after hours, Roku's net cash position including marketable securities today is slightly more moderate at around 16% of its market cap. That being said, the bulk of my contention rings true, this business operates debt free.

So, if you have a little topline growth, with strong underlying free cash flow, and good things could happen.

More specifically, Roku guides for its free cash flow in 2025 to come close to $400 million. Not only is this nearly 100% y/y increase from 2024, but crucially, look back to what my previous analysis stated:

Roku is going to finish 2024 with approximately $200 million of free cash flow. Now, let's make a bold assumption that in 2025, Roku's free cash flow resumes its climb higher so that its free cash flow is up 50% y/y to $300 million.

As you can see above, I was previously expecting to see $300 million of free cash flow.

But now, with close to $400 million of free cash flow on the cards for this year, at 35x forward free cash flow this isn't a cheap valuation, but it isn't bad either.

Ultimately, it's about investors' confidence and conviction in a business's ability to grow and deliver increasing free cash flows. And on this front, Roku is clearly delivering a solid performance.

Risk Factors to Consider

Paying up 35x forward free cash flows for Roku isn't cheap. You really need investors to continue to expect interest rates to remain steady and not increasing. Because if interest rates start to move back up, then, this multiple is too high.

The other aspect that is worthwhile thinking about is the overall ad market. Roku relies heavily on advertising, and if companies pull back on spending, that could hurt growth, as we saw with The Trade Desk (TTD). Although I would counter that TTD was way overvalued and due for a correction for some time, so one shouldn't extrapolate that insight too far.

The Bottom Line

I was wrong to be neutral on Roku.

After reviewing its latest earnings and guidance, it's clear this is a turnaround story in the making.

With free cash flow expected to nearly double in 2025, a strong balance sheet, and an expanding ad business, institutional investors are likely to take notice.

At 35x forward free cash flow and operating debt-free, Roku presents a compelling case for upside. Given these factors, I'm now bullish on the stock.

data.title: These Big Dividends Are Printing Cash - I'm Going All In
data.description:
Right now, the high-yield space is facing numerous headwinds, including elevated short- and long-term interest rates with no signs of cuts anytime soon, the threat of tariffs being placed by the Trump administration on a reciprocal basis relative to other countries’ tariffs on U.S. imports, and persistently high inflation, which has kept interest rates elevated. On top of that, geopolitical tensions have been simmering, particularly in the Far East, Middle East, and Eastern Europe.

However, there are two sectors in the high-yield space that I am very bullish on right now, as these headwinds are not nearly as impactful on them as they are for other sectors such as REITs. In this article, I will discuss why I am bullish on these two sectors, as well as some of the stocks I have been aggressively buying in them in recent months.

Sector #1

The first sector I am bullish on is infrastructure (UTF), particularly AI-related infrastructure. This sector is positioned well due to a massive tailwind from the AI boom, which is driving rapid growth in demand for data centers and the energy needed to power them. On top of that, the companies I am targeting in this sector have very strong balance sheets with built-in inflation protections and escalators in their contracts, making them more resistant to elevated inflation and interest rates. While tariffs may impact some expenses, these businesses have such strong demand for their products and services and so many ways to invest their capital that tariffs are unlikely to be a material headwind. Additionally, they have significant domestic demand and long-term, defensive contracts, meaning that geopolitical tensions should not hurt them too much. Some of my top picks in this sector include stocks in energy production, energy transportation, and data centers.

In energy production, my top pick right now is Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC). While renewable energy may be getting a bad rap in the headlines due to comments from Trump, including an executive action targeting wind power, BEP is unlikely to suffer much from this action. Only about 20% of its cash flow and development pipeline are related to wind, and a good portion of that is located outside of the United States in markets like Europe. Additionally, BEP’s crown jewel asset class is its hydropower portfolio, which consists of wide-moat, long-life assets that appreciate over time. These assets are currently benefiting from a very attractive contracting environment, providing a powerful tailwind to the company’s cash flows.

BEP also owns Westinghouse, a major player in nuclear power, which is expected to benefit significantly from the AI boom as nuclear energy becomes a key resource for powering AI infrastructure. Furthermore, BEP recently signed a massive power purchase agreement with Microsoft (MSFT) to support its AI expansion. Management has expressed confidence in securing additional contracts with other hyperscalers and potentially expanding its agreement with MSFT. Meanwhile, BEP offers a nearly 7% distribution yield and has an impressive distribution growth track record. It recently increased its distribution by 5% and expects to grow its FFO per unit at a greater than 10% CAGR for the foreseeable future. This should combine with the attractive yield to generate mid-teens annualized total returns, even before factoring in potential valuation multiple expansion. BEP’s management has also indicated that they view units as deeply undervalued and are likely to repurchase shares, further supporting the potential for multiple expansion. With a BBB+ credit rating, BEP also has a low risk balance sheet.

In the energy transportation space, my top pick is Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), which I recently wrote about in more detail. The bottom line is that EPD’s assets are well-positioned to benefit from growing energy demand by data centers in Texas. The number of data centers in Texas is expected to grow rapidly due to Project Stargate and other corporate initiatives to expand AI infrastructure across the state.

EPD boasts an A- credit rating, the best in the midstream sector, and a nearly 7% distribution yield that is covered 1.7 times by distributable cash flow. It is expected to grow its cash flows and distribution at a mid-single-digit rate for the foreseeable future while continuing its quarter-century streak of annual distribution growth.

Last but not least, Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC) has a rapidly growing data center business and is investing significant capital into it, expecting to generate very attractive returns over time. With its BBB+ credit rating, well-established capital recycling program, 5% distribution yield, and a lengthy track record of growing its distribution at a high-single-digit annual pace, BIP offers an attractive way to invest in the data center boom. Management expects FFO per unit to grow at a 10%+ CAGR for the foreseeable future, positioning it as a compelling opportunity in the AI-driven infrastructure expansion.

Sector #2

The other sector I am buying right now is gold miners (GDX), and the reason I’m so bullish on this sector is that gold (GLD) has soared over the past year, significantly boosting the profit margins of miners. Even though labor costs have increased more than they would have liked, the surge in gold prices has more than offset these expenses. Despite this, mining stocks, even among some of the highest-quality names, have not appreciated as much as they should have given the underlying growth in gold prices.

In particular, blue chips like Barrick Gold (GOLD) and Newmont Corporation (NEM), two of the largest gold miners in the world with some of the best assets, are currently trading at large discounts to their historical averages as well as at valuation multiples below those of some of their peers. Both companies are paying out solid dividend yields, aggressively buying back stock, and maintaining pristine balance sheets. Their portfolios are well-positioned for long-term growth, making them highly attractive buys right now.

These stocks look even more attractive when considering that current macroeconomic headwinds do not seem to be negatively impacting the underlying value of gold. In fact, tariffs and market unease are pushing gold prices higher, while geopolitical tensions are further supporting the rally. More importantly, the root cause of inflation—runaway government spending, particularly in the United States—is causing many global investors to lose confidence in the dollar. Many central banks are increasingly turning to gold while reducing their dollar reserves, which has provided strong support for gold prices. These trends are expected to continue for the foreseeable future, making a steep pullback in gold prices unlikely.

As a result, we see this as a very bullish environment for gold and an opportunistic time to load up on blue-chip miners trading at compelling valuations.

Investor Takeaway

As this article discusses, while dividend-paying stocks have floundered recently—evidenced by the lagging performance of the REIT sector (VNQ) and dividend growth ETFs like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) compared to the broader S&P 500—there are still sectors we are very bullish on, particularly infrastructure and gold miners.

Gold miners and infrastructure have been on pretty strong bullish runs recently, but we see these runs having significantly further to go due to the supportive macro environment and continued attractive valuations in the sector. As a result, we are buying them hand over fist at High Yield Investor.

I'm very excited to be able to recommend Brookfield again. Let me tell you the three reasons I just bought more (5X my position), and you might want to, too.

Reason One: That 15% Dividend Hike Is Just The Beginning

Brookfield's guidance is for "15%-plus dividend growth" through 2029, but they potentially have a growth runway of 15% to 20% for much longer than that.

Sector Amount Raised % of Total Notable Initiatives

Brookfield is raising record amounts ($135 billion in the last year) and has major secular trends in the trillions.

Brookfield is a world leader in some of history's biggest secular growth trends.

For example, by 2050, the total spending on green energy investments (not even mention all infrastructure) could be $300 trillion.

AI data centers by 2030 alone are a $2 trillion global investment opportunity.

As I showed in a recent Amazon (AMZN) article, the 2050 AI data center opportunity is potentially as great as $75 trillion (according to Blackstone's CEO)... and that's just for the demand from robotics and driverless cars!

  • 5 Reasons I Bought Amazon Before Earnings And So Should You

Management is also excited about infrastructure, including AI data centers. Private credit is a secular megatrend that most people don't think about, but it's massive.

Why? How will $300 trillion in green energy investments plus other infrastructure investments, plus up to $75 trillion in AI data centers, be funded?

We're talking about potentially $500 trillion in secular infrastructure investment opportunities over the next 25 years alone.

  • $20 trillion per year in opportunities...if and only if you can find the money.

Government budgets have been blown out by the Pandemic and terrible demographics (falling populations vs. pensions and safety net spending in Europe, for example), so governments can't borrow to fund this.

Private companies (like hyperscalers) are rich, but not that rich.

Big tech is expected to spend $3.5 trillion on growth over the next five years, or $700 billion annually.

Impressive right?

  • AI data centers need about $5 trillion per year through 2050 if Blackstone is right (possibly more).

The government and big tech alone can't fund it; that's where private credit is so helpful.

Private credit was $250 billion in 2008, has grown to $1.7 trillion, and is expected to double again in the next five years to about $3.5 trillion.

  • 15% CAGR growth rate (about 3X faster than global GDP).
Sector Investment Needs (Cumulative)
