Infrastructure $100–150 Trillion
Digital Infrastructure ~$10–20 Trillion
Private Credit ~$20–30 Trillion
Total Investment Opportunity $405–500 Trillion

(Source: WSJ, Kiplinker, McKinsey, Brookfield)

This isn't billions; it's trillions. Brookfield will have over $1 trillion in fee-bearing capital by 2029 (or sooner).

But with investment opportunities like this—by 2050 alone—it's not hard to imagine BAM sustaining that 15% to 20% growth rate not just for five years but possibly for decades (perhaps even a 50-year growth runway).

Reason Two: Fantastic 2024 Results

OK, we just saw some incredible and exciting potential growth runways with numbers so large they are hard to imagine.

  • $500 trillion is approximately five years of global GDP.

Brookfield's Biggest Medium-Term Growth Opportunities

Sector Annual Investment ($ Trillion)
Clean Energy >$4
Infrastructure ~$3–4
Data Centers (AI) ~$0.4
Total ~$7.4–8.4

(Source: WSJ, Kiplinker, McKinsey, Brookfield)

The investment opportunity in physical infrastructure alone is approximately $8 trillion annually in the next few years.

OK, that's a lot easier to imagine, and it shows BAM's growth potential.

But BAM just reported earnings, and while the stock price didn't show it, those results were fantastic.

In other words, BAM isn't just throwing out huge numbers and "Shark Tanking it"; they deliver on their guidance, often underpromising and overdelivering.

  • Shark Tanking: "The addressable market is $1 trillion, and if we could get just 1% of that, we'll be a $10 billion company!"

Brookfield targets $1T fee-bearing capital amid AI and clean energy growth

CEO Bruce Flatt highlighted a strong 2024 performance, with $135B raised over the year and a record $29B in Q4 organic fundraising. Fee-bearing capital grew 18% to $539B, driving $2.5B in fee-related earnings and $2.4B in distributable earnings. Strategic investments expanded credit origination capabilities and bolstered fundraising across institutional and private wealth channels." - Seeking Alpha

BAM is growing like a weed, delivering its 15% to 20% asset growth guidance mid-range, including record annual and quarterly fundraising.

  • That fund was raised to guarantee that BAM will be able to double its fee-bearing capital because of capital calls.
  • If BAM finds a good investment opportunity, it converts the capital into fee-bearing AUM and earns revenue.
  • It's contractually obligated from institutional clients.

OK, but maybe BAM issued many shares to fund this growth?

CFO Hadley Peer Marshall announced record Q4 fee-related earnings of $677M, or $0.42 per share, up 17% year-over-year. Distributable earnings reached $649M, or $0.40 per share, with strong organic capital inflows of $29B offset by $18B of returned capital. The board declared a 15% dividend increase to an annualized rate of $1.75 per share. - Seeking Alpha

Per share growth was 17%, ahead of the 14.6% current consensus and in line with management's guidance.

Combined with the new 15% higher dividend, the company has a long-term total return potential of 20% CAGR if management can keep delivering its guidance (as it has for nearly 40 years, over 20 under the current CEO).

And BAM's outlook for 2025 is strong, meaning the boom times aren't expected to end anytime soon (not until 2050, at least).

Reason Three: A Wonderful Company At A Fair Price

It's better to buy a wonderful company at a fair price, than a fair company at a wonderful price." - Warren Buffett

Am I pounding the table on Brookfield, like I recently did on Amazon (AMZN), Realty Income (O), or Nvidia (NVDA)? Nope.

It's a reasonable buy based on the best valuation model.

  • Combining its historical PE ratio since spin-off (29.6X) with its recent average dividend yield (2.67%).

Using a 12-month rolling model (86.54% weighted toward 2025 and 13.46% 2026), we can estimate Brookfield's worth at any given time based on the forward 12-month fundamentals that 80% of Wall Street's money is examining.

The 66% of fund managers who control 80% of the money on Wall Street are looking at 12-month forward fundamentals, which I also use.

  • Value is what other people are willing to pay.
  • So make sure you're estimating the same way they are.

The harmonic average of the dividend yield and PE fair value is how I estimate BAM to be worth $57.88 right now (it has increased in value every week since we shifted closer to 2026).

  • $62.15 is the end of 2025 fair value (based on 2026 consensus fundamentals).

In other words, every week, BAM's fair value rises by approximately 9 cents per share.

  • 3.0% fair value yield.
  • 33.07 fair value PE.

5 Year Total Return Potential

FAST Graphs uses a trailing blended valuation method (more conservative).

On a 12-month forward basis, the 4.5% discount to fair value means that BAM's 5-year consensus return potential is 15.5% CAGR.

That's 100% upside potential over five years, and that fair value estimate is conservative, given that BAM's fair value is likely derived from yield, not PE.

Why? It's a yield instrument explicitly created to appeal to investors who care that it pays out 95% of distributable earnings to shareholders (BN owns 73% of BAM, the parent company).

In other words, BAM is owned by those who want stable long-term income, something that no other alternative asset manager offers.

  • BX, APO, and its peers are all variable payers.
  • BAM has a progressive dividend policy.
  • This means the dividend grows in line with earnings, and management tries to avoid cutting the dividend unless absolutely necessary in a bad year.

In other words, BAM is an alternative asset manager for high-yield investors, similar to Realty Income, a "monthly dividend company."

The 2.67% average yield is likely closer to the actual fair value.

What's the fair value PE of BAM if that yield turns out to be the mean-reverting point?

  • 37.45 fair value PE if that fair value yield proves accurate over time.
  • A fair value price of $65.54 if this proves to be the actual fair value PE.
  • The potential discount could be 17% with an upside to fair value of 20%.

Risks To Consider: Why Brookfield Isn't Right For Everyone

In 2025, here are the risks that management talked about in the recent conference call.

What I'm focusing on is valuation uncertainty.

Brookfield (BN) has existed for decades (Portfolio Visualizer has data from 1985). Still, Brookfield Asset Management, the high-yield spin-off that owns all the fee-bearing assets and funds (but none of the other parts of the empire), has existed since December 2022.

In other words, all the historical data about its valuation, yield, or PE are statistically insignificant.

Why am I confident that a 2.7% fair value yield (or 3% fair value, which is DK's official estimate) is reasonable?

If you factor in the 15% to 20% CAGR management growth guidance, a yield of 2.7% to 3% is desirable.

  • 17.7% to 23% CAGR total returns over the next five years.

Buffett-like return potential is attractive for an A-rated industry leader like BAM.

In other words, let's consider the PEG rule of thumb that Graham and Peter Lynch used.

14.6% growth = 15 PE for a fair value of $26.25.

OK, why not believe that BAM is in a bubble and will eventually return to the 15 PE rule of thumb that Graham recommended for companies growing slower than 15%?

  • The formulas are built into FAST Graphs.

Because that would represent a yield of 6.7%.

That would yield higher than Realty Income for a company growing 3X to 4X faster.

  • ENB's fair value yield is 6.8%, and its growth guidance is 5%.
  • BTI's fair value yield is 6%, and its growth guidance is 5%.
  • O's fair value yield is 4.5%, and its growth consensus is 6%.

Granted, those are low-volatility dividend aristocrats.

But as BAM proves that its "annuity-like" asset base and cash flows permit highly stable income even in bear markets (which shouldn't affect its business), I'm confident that BAM's fair value yield will prove to be around 2.5% to 3%.

Vanguard's High Dividend Yield ETF (VYM) yields 2.8% and SCHD yields 3.5%.

50% to 200% faster growth than lower volatility peers seems reasonable, but that's speculative.

Ultimately, we won't be able to say what BAM's fair value PE or yield truly is for a long time.

  • 90% statistical confidence in December 2032.
  • 91% statistical confidence in December 2042.
  • 97% statistical confidence in December 2052.

If BAM's fair value PE is 15X to 25X, multiple contractions will consume much of its future growth.

The plus side? The yield is independent of PE, and as long as the dividend remains stable, as management expects, any declines will make BAM a more attractive long-term income growth investment.

But there's no guarantee that BAM's highly aggressive payout policy (95% payout ratio target and consensus payout ratio for 2026 is 88%) is sustainable.

Theoretically, with years of guaranteed growth built in (via capital calls), BAM's dividend can be sustained even in a negative growth year (which shouldn't happen).

It has an A-credit rating, so it can borrow to pay the dividend for a year or two without risking its balance sheet.

However, theoretically, if a global crisis occurred worse than the GFC, it might have to cut its dividend.

I wouldn't necessarily automatically sell BAM if it cuts the dividend in the future, and analysts don't expect it to (though the consensus estimates only go out to 2027). However, analysts can always be proven wrong.

So far, BAM has never missed EPS expectations, though the historical data is so short that we have only one point of reference.

Bottom Line: I Trust Brookfield For Decades To Come, And You Can Too (I'm 80% Certain Of It)

According to John Templeton and Howard Marks, "20% of the time, this time really is different."

In other words, even if 100% of the data today says a company appears to be "perfect" and a "sure thing," there's always a 20% chance that the fundamentals might collapse later and prove your investment thesis wrong.

  • Thus, what I call the 80% Templeton/Marks certainty limit for any individual company.
  • Also, why do I use a 20% or less Maximum risk cap on even the highest-quality companies?
  • (I'm willing to overweight 10% to 22% if the valuation is strong enough).

Can I say with certainty that Brookfield is an excellent long-term holding for the next two decades? No. I can say with 80% confidence that anyone buying today, with a long-term time frame (such as 10-plus years), is 90% likely to be very happy.

That time frame represents the "long term," when fundamentals dominate and luck takes care of itself.

If you aren't willing to own a stock for 10 years, don't even think about owning it for 10 minutes." - Warren Buffett

In other words, I can say with 72% statistical certainty that buying Brookfield today, if you're OK with its irrational short-term volatility (and overall risk profile), is a smart long-term decision for anyone willing to own it until 2035.

And that's good enough for me to 5X my position and set aggressive limits for a potential correction that might buy me another 9,000 shares (increasing my position to approximately 9% of the portfolio).

That's not a prediction that BAM might fall 20% in the coming weeks, but if it does, I'm ready to make my own luck.

data.title: Gold-Silver Ratio: Silver Breakout Incoming?
data.description:
By Peter Schiff

Gold has reached new all-time highs, and the gold-to-silver ratio now exceeds 90:1. Such a high ratio has often signaled an impending breakout for silver prices. It indicates that silver could be undervalued, and we may be on the verge of a major price surge. Sometimes, if you miss a price run-up for gold, you can make up for it by buying silver instead as it catches up to its yellow cousin.

The gold-to-silver ratio measures how many ounces of silver it takes to buy one ounce of gold. Over the past century, this ratio has typically ranged between 40:1 and 70:1. When it rises above 80:1, silver has often experienced sharp upward movements in price. Currently, with gold soaring past previous highs and silver lagging behind, the imbalance suggests that silver could be due for a strong rally.

That’s especially true as gold is unlikely to be done with its trend of hitting all-time highs this year, and may surge further into the stratosphere. Now, silver is edging back up - are we about to witness an epic breakout?

Gold-Silver Ratio, 1-Week

History shows that extreme gold-to-silver ratios have frequently preceded silver bull runs. For example:

  • 1991: The ratio spiked to nearly 100:1 before silver surged by over 50% in the following years.
  • 2003: The ratio stood at 80:1 before silver began a multi-year rally, climbing from $4.50 per ounce to over $20 by 2008.
  • 2008-2011: After the financial crisis, the ratio hit about 83:1, and silver rocketed from $9 per ounce to nearly $50 by 2011.
  • 2020: The ratio briefly exceeded an incredible 120:1 during the pandemic market crash, but silver rebounded from $12 to over $28 within months.

Each of these instances followed a pattern: when the ratio climbed to extreme levels, silver eventually outperformed gold in percentage terms as it caught up, even though gold tends to outperform silver in the longer-run.

Several factors support the case for a silver breakout. The first is monetary policy and inflation. Even as Jerome Powell announces a pause to rate cuts to tamper down inflation, the money supply finds a way to expand. When central banks continue to print money, it fuels inflation and increasing demand for hard assets. Gold’s rally suggests that investors are seeking protection against devaluation, and silver, historically a store of value, tends to follow gold’s lead with higher volatility.

Industrial demand for silver is also widespread. Silver’s role extends beyond investment; it is a key component in solar panels, electronics, and electric vehicles. With the global push toward green energy, demand for silver in industrial applications is rising. The Silver Institute forecasts industrial silver demand to remain buoyed by growing industrial need in the coming years, with 2025 marking the fifth consecutive year of demand outpacing supply even as it declines for jewelry and other uses.

Unlike gold, much of the silver mined is consumed in industrial processes, making above-ground supplies more limited. Primary silver mines are scarce, and supply chains have faced disruptions in recent years. Silver also remains historically undervalued compared to gold. As more investors recognize this discrepancy, demand for silver could surge, driving prices higher.

Silver vs USD 1-Month

If history repeats itself, a breakout in silver could happen swiftly. When silver moves, it often does so aggressively. In past rallies, it has outpaced gold’s gains on a percentage basis, rewarding investors who positioned themselves early.

With gold reaching new highs and the gold-to-silver ratio at extreme levels, conditions appear ripe for silver to follow suit. After all, gold isn’t all that glitters - keep an eye on silver in the coming months as investors watch Trump tariffs, Fed decisions, and the state of multiple global conflicts in Gaza and Ukraine as the Trump administration enters full swing.
